text: holidays. Durkheim argued that symbols function to give sentiments and social moods a lasting social life. Without symbols, ‘social feelings could have only an unstable existence’ (Durkheim 1995: 232; it is partly this perspective that Alexander (2006) develops in his performance approach to social events, similarly invoking political revolutions as examples). Implicitly, Durkheim thus endorsed the French Revolution as an emblematic political moment of collective effervescence on the basis of which the national community was given life, with the lasting symbols of liberté, egalité and fraternité (ibid.: 429–30; see discussion by Tiryakian 2009, Chapter 10). The stamping of the new conscience collective also took place via instituting national holidays that, as in the ritual cycle of the Australian aborigines, kept celebrating itself: ‘We have already seen how the French Revolution established a whole cycle of celebrations in order to keep the principles that inspired it eternally young’ (Durkheim 1995: 430). These direct and indirect allusions to the revolution are made in the concluding chapter of Durkheim’s concluding book, in the crucial section on That Which is Eternal about Religion. Durkheim simply did not celebrate the revolution, but it is clear that Durkheim came to see political revolutions as potential replacements of religion. At the least, something is needed, as ‘the former gods are growing old or dying, and others have not been born’ (ibid.: 429). He indicates that there will be, must be, new revivals of society’s ‘original moment’: ‘There are no immortal gospels, and there is there is no reason to believe that humanity is incapable of conceiving new ones in the future’ (ibid.: 430). Indeed, Durkheim is almost wishing himself into the revolutionary future: ‘A day will come when our societies once again will know hours of creative effervescence, during which new ideas will spring forth and new formulae emerge to guide humanity’ (ibid.: 429). Durkheim was attracted to the idea of revolution in exactly this sense – and Mauss was reflexively aware of this. 7 7 Arnold van Gennep did not approach the theme of revolution directly either. However, in his work on nationalism, he more than indicated that revolutions simply Liminality and the Modern 200 It is in this light that Mauss felt ‘compelled’ to provide an analysis of the Bolshevik experiment, launching a devastating critique. The startling conclusion is that there was a line of influence from Durkheim to Lenin. Mauss sums it up nicely: The idea and the realisation of the soviet corresponded – to the very image – with two of the few moral, political and economic conclusions that Durkheim had always advocated and that death had prevented him seeing actually materialised. The whole conclusion of both the Social Division of Labour and of his Suicide, all his teachings on civic, professional and domestic morals, advocated both the constitution of this professional property and the establishment of a moral and political law of the group formed out of the economic association of those united in the same production. Even the purely scientific conclusions of his lectures, his History of the Family, led him to make the professional group, if not the universal legatee, at least the partial inheritor of the rights, duties and political powers of the ancient family. (Mauss 1992: 172) What Mauss emphasizes here is the surprising closeness of Durkheim’s theory and the practice of the soviets. Moreover, in some of his letters written in the mid-1930s, Mauss would extend his reflections towards a deeper understanding of communism, fascism and Nazism. Here again one must understand Mauss correctly. Mauss is evidently not saying that Durkheim himself would have been a direct supporter of any such -ism; such a claim would be an absurdity indeed. What he says goes deeper, and it concerns the authority – analytical and political – given to the collective level. The institutional vector though which this came to operate in effective history was corporatism, as this also develops from what Mauss calls ‘active minorities’ and their use of violence. In his significant letter to Ranulf from 1936 (in Gane 1992: 214–15), Mauss traces this corporatism back to Sorel and forwards to Lenin and Mussolini, but then notes how Sorel was himself inspired by Durkheim. Mauss and Durkheim had known Sorel since 1893, and he was one main medium through which Durkheim’s collectivist approach inspired leading socialists and revolutionary syndicalism. It should not be forgotten – and Mauss had not forgotten – that when facing the challenge of anomie, as in the concluding discussion of Suicide, Durkheim did not simply advocate a return to tradition, family or religion; quite the contrary: it was with the occupational group and its future organization that his hope of overcoming anomie rested. By the 1930s Mauss had fully realized the ‘tragic irony’ involved. They, the founders of the theory of collective representation, ‘were satisfied with a few allusions to crowd states, when something quite different was at stake’, could not be the founding moments of nations or communities. Nations, to van Gennep, were meaningful ethnographic entities to the extent that they grew out of a long history of concrete practices, ways of life and idioms passed on through generations (see in particular his discussion of ‘linguistic symbols’, Chapter IV in van Gennep 1922). Liminal Politics: Towards an Anthropology of Political Revolutions 201 as he wrote in a second letter to Ranulf (ibid.: 214). That great modern societies, emerging from the Middle Ages, could be made ‘to turn around like children in a ring’ was something that Mauss and Durkheim simply had not been able to grasp. They had wrongly and fatally sought to demonstrate that ‘it was in the collective mind that the individual could find the basis and sustenance for his freedom, his independence, his personality and his critical faculties’ (ibid.). What Mauss saw unfolding in Bolshevik Russia and in interwar Europe was to him a ‘real tragedy ... an unwelcome verification of the things we had been suggesting and the proof that we should perhaps have expected this verification in
